subject: Chemistry
q: A student needs to prepare a buffer solution of propanoic acid and its sodium salt with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mn>4</mn></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msup><mi>COO</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced></mfrac></math> required to make buffer is Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>
question: A student needs to prepare a buffer solution of propanoic acid and its sodium salt with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mn>4</mn></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msup><mi>COO</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced></mfrac></math> required to make buffer is Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>
solution:
Henderson's equation for calculating pH of acidic buffer solutions is given as

pH=pKa+logSaltAcid

pH=pKa+logCH3COO-CH3COOH

4=5-log(1.3)+logCH3COO-CH3COOH

log1.3×10-1=logCH3COO-CH3COOH

CH3COO-CH3COOH=0.13

subject: Chemistry
q:
A soft drink was bottled with a partial pressure of CO2 of 3 bar over the liquid at room temperature. The partial pressure of CO2 over the solution approaches a value of 30 bar when 44 g of CO2 is dissolved in 1 kg of water at room temperature. The approximate pH of the soft drink is ___________×10-1.
(First dissociation constant of H2CO3=4.0×10-7; log2=0.3; density of the soft drink =1 g mL−1)
question:
A soft drink was bottled with a partial pressure of CO2 of 3 bar over the liquid at room temperature. The partial pressure of CO2 over the solution approaches a value of 30 bar when 44 g of CO2 is dissolved in 1 kg of water at room temperature. The approximate pH of the soft drink is ___________×10-1.
(First dissociation constant of H2CO3=4.0×10-7; log2=0.3; density of the soft drink =1 g mL−1)
solution:
Amount of CO2 in one liter of solution =4.4 . =0.1 Mole

pH=1/2{pKa-logC}

For a weak acid solution

pH=1/2{6.4+1}=3.7

subject: Chemistry
q: For a sparingly soluble salt \(\mathrm{AB}_2\), the equilibrium concentrations of \(\mathrm{A}^{2+}\) ions and \(B^{-}\)ions are \(1.2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}\) and \(0.24 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\), respectively. The solubility product of \(\mathrm{AB}_2\) is :
question: For a sparingly soluble salt \(\mathrm{AB}_2\), the equilibrium concentrations of \(\mathrm{A}^{2+}\) ions and \(B^{-}\)ions are \(1.2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}\) and \(0.24 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}\), respectively. The solubility product of \(\mathrm{AB}_2\) is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AB}_{2(\mathrm{~s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{~B}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \\ & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{A}^{+2}\right]\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]^2 \\ &=1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times\left(2.4 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2 \\ &=6.91 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{M}^3\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>OH</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of ammonium acetate solution will be
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>OH</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of ammonium acetate solution will be
solution:
Ka of CH3COOH=1.8×10-5

Ka of NH4OH=1.8×10-5

We know that pH of solution of the salt made up of weak acid CH3COOH and weak base NH4OH can be found using formula

pH=7+12pKa-pKb

pKa=-logKa
=-log1.8×10-5=-log 1.8+log 10-5=-0.2553-5=+4.7447pKb=-logKb=-log1.8×10-5=4.7447pH=7+124.7447-4.7447   =7+0   =7

If Ka=Kb, then pH of salt is always 7.
Therefore, the pH of given solution is 7.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following arrangements represents the correct order of the proton affinity of the given species :
question: Which one of the following arrangements represents the correct order of the proton affinity of the given species :
solution:
The species with the greatest proton affinity will be the strongest base, and its conjugate acid will be the weakest acid. The weakest acid will have the smallest value of $\mathrm{K}_a$. Since $\mathrm{HI}$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{HF}$ which is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{S}$, a partial order of proton affinity is 
$\mathrm{I}^{-} < \mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{HS}^{-}$
Since $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is a very weak acid, $\mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$must be a very strong base. Therefore the correct order of proton affinity is
$\mathrm{I}^{-} < \mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{HS}^{-} < \mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{OH}^{-}$solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
question: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{OH}^{-}$solution at $330 \mathrm{~K}$, if $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}$ at $330 \mathrm{~K}$ is $10^{-13.6}$ ?
solution:
Given at $330 \mathrm{~K}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{K}_w & =10^{-13.6} \\
\text { i.e. } \quad \mathrm{pK}_w & =\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\because \quad \mathrm{pOH}^2 & =-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\
13.6 & =\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH} \\
\mathrm{pOH} & =-\log 10^{-4} \\
13.6 & =\mathrm{pH}+4 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH} & =13.6-4 \\
& =9.6
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Aqueous solution of ammonium carbonate is basic.
Statement (II) : Acidic/basic nature of salt solution of a salt of weak acid and weak base depends on Ka and Kb value of acid and the base forming it.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Aqueous solution of ammonium carbonate is basic.
Statement (II) : Acidic/basic nature of salt solution of a salt of weak acid and weak base depends on Ka and Kb value of acid and the base forming it.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
solution:
Aqueous solution of (NH4)2CO3 is Basic.

pH of salt of weak acid and weak base depends on Ka and Kb value of acid and the base forming it.

Solution is basic if

Kb (weak base) >Ka (weak acid).

Ka (HCO3-)=4.3×10−13

Kb(NH4OH)=1.8×10−5

Kb (Ammonium hydroxide) >Ka (Hydrogen carbonate)

∴ Hence, solution of Ammonium carbonate is basic in nature.

Hence Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: Class <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>XII</mi></math> students were asked to prepare one litre of buffer solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math> by their chemistry teacher. The amount of ammonium chloride to be dissolved by the student in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> ammonia solution to make one litre of the buffer is (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>pK</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>74</mn><mo>;</mo></math> Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>53</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Class <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>XII</mi></math> students were asked to prepare one litre of buffer solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math> by their chemistry teacher. The amount of ammonium chloride to be dissolved by the student in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> ammonia solution to make one litre of the buffer is (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>pK</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>74</mn><mo>;</mo></math> Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>53</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
A mixture of NH3 and NH4Cl will form a basic buffer.

pH of final solution =8.26

pOH of final solution=14-8.26 =5.74

Let the number of moles of NH4Cl added to 1 L solution of be x.

Using Henderson’s equation.

pOH=pKb+logNH4ClNH3

5.74=4.74+logx0.2

∴ x=2

The amount of ammonium chloride to be dissolved by the student in 0.2M ammonia solution to make one litre of buffer=2×53.5=107g

subject: Chemistry
q: The $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of a weak acid, $\mathrm{HA}$, is $4.80$. The $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of a weak base, $\mathrm{BOH}$, is $4.78$. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of an aqueous solution of the corresponding salt, BA, will be
question: The $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of a weak acid, $\mathrm{HA}$, is $4.80$. The $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of a weak base, $\mathrm{BOH}$, is $4.78$. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of an aqueous solution of the corresponding salt, BA, will be
solution:
It is a salt of weak acid and weak base
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}}} \\
& \mathrm{pH}=7.01
\end{aligned}
$$

The<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of butyric acid is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Nearest integer) [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn></math>]
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub></math> for butyric acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of butyric acid is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Nearest integer) [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn></math>]
solution:
Ka of Butyric acid ⇒2×10-5       pKa=-logKa = 4.7

pH of weak acid solution

pH=12pKa-12logC

=124·7-12log0.2

=2.35+0.35=2.7

pH=27×10-1

question: Hydrogen ion concentration in mol / L in a solution of pH = 5.4 will be
solution: $\mathrm{p}^{H}=-\log \left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$

subject: Chemistry
q: 25.0 mL of 0.050 M BaNO32 is mixed with 25.0 mL of 0.020 M NaF.Ksp of BaF2 is 0.5×10–6 at 298K. The ratio of Ba2+F-2 and Ksp is ______.
question: 25.0 mL of 0.050 M BaNO32 is mixed with 25.0 mL of 0.020 M NaF.Ksp of BaF2 is 0.5×10–6 at 298K. The ratio of Ba2+F-2 and Ksp is ______.
solution:
Given:

Volume of Ba(NO3)2 solution = 25.0mL  Concentration of Ba(NO3)2 solution = 0.050M Volume of NaF solution = 25.0 mL  Concentration of NaF solution = 0.020M

Ba+2=25×0.0550=0.025M

F-=25×0.0250=0.01M

Ba+2 F-=25×10-7

Ksp=0.5×10-6=5×10-7

Ratio=Ba+2F-Ksp=25×10-75×10-7=5

subject: Chemistry
q: The solubility product constants of $Ag_2CrO_4$ and $AgBr$ are $32x$ and $4y$ respectively at $298$ K. The value of $\left(\dfrac{\text{molarity of } Ag_2CrO_4}{\text{molarity of } AgBr}\right)$ can be expressed as :
question: The solubility product constants of $Ag_2CrO_4$ and $AgBr$ are $32x$ and $4y$ respectively at $298$ K. The value of $\left(\dfrac{\text{molarity of } Ag_2CrO_4}{\text{molarity of } AgBr}\right)$ can be expressed as :
solution:
Let the solubility of $Ag_2CrO_4$ be $S_1$.

For $Ag_2CrO_4 \rightleftharpoons 2Ag^+ + CrO_4^{2-}$

$K_{sp} = (2S_1)^2(S_1) = 4S_1^3$

$4S_1^3 = 32x \Rightarrow S_1^3 = 8x \Rightarrow S_1 = 2\sqrt[3]{x}$

Let the solubility of $AgBr$ be $S_2$.

For $AgBr \rightleftharpoons Ag^+ + Br^-$

$K_{sp} = S_2^2$

$S_2^2 = 4y \Rightarrow S_2 = 2\sqrt{y}$

The ratio of their molarities is:

$\dfrac{S_1}{S_2} = \dfrac{2\sqrt[3]{x}}{2\sqrt{y}} = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{x}}{\sqrt{y}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\sqrt[3]{x}}{\sqrt{y}}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the dissociation equilibrium of the following weak acid
$\mathrm{HA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{A}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
If the pKa of the acid is 4, then the pH of 10 mMHA solution is $\_\_\_\_$.(Nearest integer)
[Given: The degree of dissociation can be neglected with respect to unity]
question:
Consider the dissociation equilibrium of the following weak acid
$\mathrm{HA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{A}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
If the pKa of the acid is 4, then the pH of 10 mMHA solution is $\_\_\_\_$.(Nearest integer)
[Given: The degree of dissociation can be neglected with respect to unity]
solution:
For the weak acid dissociation $HA \rightleftharpoons H^+ + A^-$ with pKa = 4
We have Ka = 10⁻⁴.
For a 10 mM (0.01 M) solution where the degree of dissociation is negligible with respect to unity, the concentration of HA remains approximately 0.01 M.
The equilibrium expression gives
$Ka = \frac{[H^+]^2}{[HA]}$
Substituting: $10^{-4} = \frac{[H^+]^2}{0.01}$, which yields $[H^+]^2 = 10^{-6}$, so $[H^+] = 10^{-3}$ M.
Therefore, pH = $-\log(10^{-3}) = 3$.

q: The solubility product of a salt having general formula $M X_2$, in water is: $4 \times 10^{-12}$. The concentration of $\mathrm{M}^{2+}$ ions in the aqueous solution of the salt is
question: The solubility product of a salt having general formula $M X_2$, in water is: $4 \times 10^{-12}$. The concentration of $\mathrm{M}^{2+}$ ions in the aqueous solution of the salt is
solution: $\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 \mathrm{~s}^3, \mathrm{~S}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{4}}=1 \times 10^{-4}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
At 298 K, the solubility of silver chloride in water is 1.434×10-3 g L-1. The value of -logKsp for silver chloride is
(Given mass of Ag is 107.9 g mol-1, and mass of Cl is 35.5 g mol-1 )
question:
At 298 K, the solubility of silver chloride in water is 1.434×10-3 g L-1. The value of -logKsp for silver chloride is
(Given mass of Ag is 107.9 g mol-1, and mass of Cl is 35.5 g mol-1 )
solution:
Formula unit mass of Silver chloride = 108 + 35.5 gmol-1 = 143.5 gmol-1
Solubility(s)=1.434143.4×10-3=10-5M

∴Ksp(AgCl)=[Ag][Cl] =(s)2

⇒Ksp(AgCl)=10-10

⇒-logKsp=10

subject: Chemistry

(Integer answer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is a sparingly soluble salt of molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> and solubility <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The solubility product satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> is __________ . =3322xm5

subject: Chemistry
q: The first and second ionization constants of a weak dibasic acid $H_2 A$ are $8.1 \times 10^{-8}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. $0.1$ mol of $H_2 A$ was dissolved in $1$ L of $0.1$ M HCl solution. The concentration of $HA^-$ in the resultant solution is:
question: The first and second ionization constants of a weak dibasic acid $H_2 A$ are $8.1 \times 10^{-8}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. $0.1$ mol of $H_2 A$ was dissolved in $1$ L of $0.1$ M HCl solution. The concentration of $HA^-$ in the resultant solution is:
solution:
Given:
Weak dibasic acid $H_2A$ with $K_{a1} = 8.1 \times 10^{-8}$ and $K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}$
Initial concentration: $[H_2A]_0 = 0.1\text{ M}$
The solution also contains $0.1\text{ M HCl}$, which dissociates completely to give $[H^+]_0 = 0.1\text{ M}$.

The main source of $HA^-$ is the first dissociation:
$H_2A \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HA^-$

Let $x$ be the amount of $H_2A$ that dissociates. Then at equilibrium:
$[H_2A] = 0.1 - x$
$[H^+] = 0.1 + x$
$[HA^-] = x$

Applying the equilibrium expression:
$K_{a1} = \dfrac{[H^+][HA^-]}{[H_2A]}$

$8.1 \times 10^{-8} = \dfrac{(0.1 + x)(x)}{0.1 - x}$

Since $K_{a1}$ is very small and the common $H^+$ ion from HCl suppresses dissociation, $x \ll 0.1$. Hence:
$0.1 - x \approx 0.1$ and $0.1 + x \approx 0.1$

$8.1 \times 10^{-8} \approx \dfrac{(0.1)(x)}{0.1} = x$

Therefore:
$[HA^-] = x = 8.1 \times 10^{-8}\text{ M}$

The second dissociation ($K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}$) is negligible compared to the first, so the $HA^-$ consumed in that step is insignificant.

Hence, the correct option is $(3)\ 8.1 \times 10^{-8}\text{ M}$.

q: Addition of sodium hydroxide solution to a weak acid (HA) results in a buffer of pH 6. If ionization constant of HA is <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the ratio of salt to acid concentration in the buffer solution will be:

subject: Chemistry
q: Assuming that the degree of hydrolysis is small, the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium acetate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.0 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ will be:
question: Assuming that the degree of hydrolysis is small, the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium acetate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.0 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ will be:
solution:
Sodium acetate is a salt of strong base and weak acid.
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\therefore \mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{p} K_a+\frac{1}{2} \log c \quad \text { where } \\
&\mathrm{p} k_a=-\log K_a=-\log 10^{-5}=5 \\
&\log \mathrm{c}=\log 10^{-1}=-1 \\
&\mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{5}{2}-\frac{1}{2}=9.0
\end{aligned}
$$

at equivalence point

Let molarity of Na2CO3

solution =M, then

M=0.05 mol/L

subject: Chemistry
q: A weak acid HA has degree of dissociation x . Which option gives the correct expression of ( pH $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ )?
question: A weak acid HA has degree of dissociation x . Which option gives the correct expression of ( pH $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ )?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right](\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{C}(1-\mathrm{x})} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \frac{(1-\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{x}}} \\ & \log \mathrm{H}^{+}=\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \left(\frac{1-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \\ & -\log \mathrm{H}^{+}=-\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}-\log \frac{1-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}-\log \frac{1-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \mathrm{pH}-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=\log \frac{\mathrm{x}}{1-\mathrm{x}}\end{aligned}$

q:
Arrange the following in increasing order of solubility product :
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2, \mathrm{AgBr}, \mathrm{PbS}, \mathrm{HgS}$
question:
Arrange the following in increasing order of solubility product :
$\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2, \mathrm{AgBr}, \mathrm{PbS}, \mathrm{HgS}$
solution:
Based on the Ksp values and salt analysis cation identification, we can say that order of Ksp value is:
$\mathrm{HgS} < \mathrm{PbS} < \mathrm{AgBr} < \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2$

K sp values
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{HgS} \rightarrow 4 \times 10^{-53} \\
& \mathrm{PbS} \rightarrow 8 \times 10^{-28} \\
& \mathrm{AgBr} \rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-13} \\
& \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2 \rightarrow 5.5 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Assertion: The amphoteric behaviour of water is explained by Lewis acid base theory

Reason: water acts as acid with NH3 and base with H2S
question:
Assertion: The amphoteric behaviour of water is explained by Lewis acid base theory

Reason: water acts as acid with NH3 and base with H2S
options: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.
solution:
H2O⏟Acid+N¨H3⏟Base→OH-+NH4+

H2O⏟Base +H2SAcid⟶H3O++HS⊖

The amphoteric behaviour of H2O is explained by Brønsted theory not the Lewis acid base theory.

So, we can say Assertion is false, but reason is true.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider two Group IV metal ions $\mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Y}^{2+}$. A solution containing $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Y}^{2+}$ is saturated with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$. The pH at which the metal sulphide YS will form as a precipitate is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{XS})=1 \times 10^{-22}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{YS})=4 \times 10^{-16}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right]=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ in solution, $\mathrm{K}_{a 1} \times \mathrm{K}_{a 2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right)=1.0 \times 10^{-21}, \log 2=0.30$, $\log 3=0.48, \log 5=0.70$)
question:
Consider two Group IV metal ions $\mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Y}^{2+}$. A solution containing $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Y}^{2+}$ is saturated with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$. The pH at which the metal sulphide YS will form as a precipitate is $\_\_\_\_$. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{XS})=1 \times 10^{-22}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{YS})=4 \times 10^{-16}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right]=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ in solution, $\mathrm{K}_{a 1} \times \mathrm{K}_{a 2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right)=1.0 \times 10^{-21}, \log 2=0.30$, $\log 3=0.48, \log 5=0.70$)
solution:
$XS(s) \rightleftharpoons X^{+2}(aq.) + S^{2-}(aq.)$
For precipitation of $XS(s)$
$[X^{+2}][S^{2-}] \geq K_{sp}(XS)$
$[S^{2-}] \geq \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-22}}{0.01} = 10^{-20}$
$YS(s) \rightleftharpoons Y^{+2}(aq) + S^{2-}(aq)$
For precipitation of $YS(s)$
$[Y^{+2}][S^{2-}] \geq K_{sp}(YS)$
$[S^{2-}] \geq \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-2}} = 4 \times 10^{-14}$
Now, $H_2S(aq) \rightleftharpoons 2H^+(aq) + S^{2-}(aq)$
$\dfrac{[S^{2-}][H^+]^2}{H_2S} = K_{a_1} \times K_{a_2} = 1 \times 10^{-21}$
$[S^{2-}] = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-21} \times [H_2S]}{[H^+]^2} \geq 4 \times 10^{-14}$
$[H^+]^2 \leq \dfrac{1}{4} \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{-1}$
$[H^+] \leq \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-4} \Rightarrow pH \geq 4.3$
Nearest integer pH = 4.

subject: Chemistry
q: The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> will be maximum in which of the following?
question: The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> will be maximum in which of the following?
solution:
Due to the common ion effect of chloride, the solubility of silver chloride is less in HCl and KCl, compared to solubility in water. In deionized water no common ion effect will take place so maximum solubility

AgCl(s)⇌Ag+(aq)+Cl-(aq)
